CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The inefficiency and weakness of the U.I.L. and of
the Irish Party provide the Enniscorthy performers
with 2 brief day upon the talking stage. The political
centre of gravity has shifted from the heart of the
country towards the towns, and any political Party
attempting to balance itself on the assumption that the
centrs of gravity is where it was thirty years ago, are
attempting the impossible. The political Party of the
future must altract the interest of the townsman. The
Irish city working man has been so long uninterested
in Irish politics that he is hitched on largely to English
labour centres now. As for the manufacturers—what
interest have they in the United Irish League or in the
Irish Parliamentary Party? The Irish situation calls
for three classes of efforts—one is the increase cf Irish

" productivity all round in agricultural and manufactured
‘ goods. It calls for a readjustment of our taxation, and

it calls for freedom, legislative and administrative, to
exert our own Irish common sense on our own affairs.
How far the latter freedom should go appears to us
rather an academic question that may be loft as a sub-
ject of excited discussion to those who like these sort

Now an Organisation and Parliamentary Party that
will gain the support of the farmers who want to make
the most of their industry on its productive and dis-
tributive sides, and the support of the manufacturers
and business people, must be governed by level-headed
men rather than by extreme ‘‘heroes,”” or by dema-
gogues who may be effective enough for leading a strike,
or an attack on such an institution as landlordism as it
was known thirty years ago.

A level-headed Party is the only one that can be
really effective; the country will not respond to any
other. ‘ Extremists’’ like Alderman Kelly—a man
whose qualities of heart we feel sure compensate largely
for his lack of sense—may kick up rows in the Cor-
poration, and make speeches to loud and prolonged
cheering youngsters at some juvenile revolutionary and
literary society; but that sort of thing doesn’t count
except as ‘“copy”’ for the evening papers. It moves
nothing, carries nothing positive; it only tends to keep
level-headed men in a state of shaking their heads at
Irish politics.

Having satisfied ourselves that a political Party,
governed by level-headed men, is the need of the times,
the practical question arises how to bring it into exis-
tence. Many people suggest a conference. Well, a
conference would not do much harm if it did no good.
We suspect that Redmond, Dillon, and the ‘‘Freeman’’
won’t have a conference; we likewise suspect that Wil-
liam O’Brien, though he might successfully hide the
fact from himself, would attempt to use a conference to
down the ““ Freeman’’ Party and get on’top himself.
Mr. O’Brien, unfortunately, has not a level head, and
his ultra-emotional temperament unfits him for the
present situation. The great effect which the “ little
Leaper *’ produced in Ireland in a short time indicates
what power the metropolitan daily press wields, or
rather could wield. The ‘‘ Freeman’’ and ‘‘ Indepen-
dent *’ are’ merely wet blankets on Ireland, their in-
fluence is. largely negative, and they had to be driven
by forces worked up, not only without their aid, but
in spite of them, to their milk-and-water Irish-Ireland
altitude. If we had a daily LeADER the country would
soon swing round and make rapid progress ahead. But
wo haven’t the money to start a daily paper, so that
solution is not within the region of practical politics.

One never knows what utterly unexpected thing may
happen, but barring accidents, so far as we can see
ahead, this country will muddle along for a few years
without any great changes. The Irish Party, as con-
trolled by Redmond, Dillon, T. P. O’Connor, and the
‘“ Freeman,’”’ may manage to keep the political pot
simmering, but that is about all. The country, we
venture to say, will never put its trust in that com-
bination. Wm. O’Brien is likewise out of the ques-
tion. Tim Healy, had he the ambition, and did he put
his heart into it, is about the only politician of the old
order that would stand a chance, for he never got his
chance' and failed. But, then, they say that Tim is
making any amount of thousands at the Bar, and we
doubt if one with so few, if any, illusions as the mem-
ber .for Louth could pump up enough of the fanatic
at this time of his life to go before the country with a

constructive workable programme. He is bitterly hated
by many, but that doesn’t count for much—we are
hated ourselves and we thrive on it. The man that
can do the work in any particular situation is generally
allowed to do it, and Ireland is so bankrupt at present
in the matter of first-class politicians that it would
welcome anyono who had not failed. ~Mr. Edward
Martyn’s pantomime will lose its novelty shortly, even
with the inexperienced young men who think that in-
tense ““ yearnings”’ and ‘“soulful”’ spasms will create
industry and foster trade.

It may not be “soulful”’ to say so, and the ““opal
hush ”’ poets may be shocked, but you want an econo-
mic motive behind political movements. It was, with
all due respect to Parnell, the economic motive that
made the Land League formidable. .The ‘“‘overworked’’
Trish police are mnow agitating—we suppose the
paddler we saw mnear Dripsey is one of the
most  enthusiastic—there is quite a sentimental
flavour in some of the messages.  ‘“ Comrades’’
and such terms almost make one think of the bobbies
as if they were Fenians or ’98 men. Yet what is the
whole thing founded on? The ‘‘comrades’’ part is
only the embroidery; the real point is the bobbies
want a rise in their wages! i

If political organisation and Members of Parliament
are to be made interesting to the manufacturers, arti-
sans and farmers of this country, there must be some
sort of a rise of wages in prospect. When you have
that solid material prospect before the people you can
talk plenty of sentiment and patriotism, and the manu-
facturers and farmers will be very pleased at being told
that they are patriots, as no doubt, to a very consider-
able extent, they would be, and are willing to be. But
business is business.

A worker in the language movement writes to us
asking that we ‘““try and shame some of our pro-
fessional prize winners at Feiseanna out of their greed
for prizes.”” He says:—‘‘ There are men who hap-
pened to be born with good voices and learned a few
Irish songs, little boys and little girls, lithe of limb,
who were trained to do a few steps of a hornpipe, and
such-like people who have made themselves actual eye-
sores at Feisanna to the earnest Gaelic Leaguer who
works to revive his or her native language. Let the
Leaper curl its lip at them; they are not worth the
lash.”

We have no information as to the extent of the
nuisance of which our correspondent complains. The
primary object of local Feiseanna is to encourage the
Trish-Ireland movement in the several localities, and
not to offer rewards to greedy competitors. The
nuisance, it seems to us, can be easily put .an end to
by limiting the area in which competitors are eligible,
and disqualifying those who have won a prize or a
specified number of prizes at similar competitions.

The British Postal Dump that was to have heen
dumped into Castlerea has declined. We read that the
appointment of Mr. H. W. Cooper, salaried Sub-Post-
master of Ilkeston, Nottingham, to be Postmaster of
Castlerea, has been cancelled at his own request. We
suppose the poor Dump got into a funk at all the
notice that was being taken of him in the Lraper. Mr.
G. Treacey, Postmaster of Edgeworthstown, has since
been appointed to the Castlerea vacancy.

THE LEADER.

Avcust 10, 1907,

Our recent article, ‘‘An Impression of Corlg,’”’ liag
inspired a Cork writer to give his impressions of the
Leaper and its Editor. We are too modest to prin
what he says of the latter, but we let him have his say
on the influence of the LEADER:—‘ Our thoughts went,
back to the days before the LEADER came on the
scene,” and we remembered the Gaelic League as it then
was. In a small backroom away up near the roof in
the Young Men’s Society ip Castle Street, eight or ten
men, some young enthusiasts, and some  greyhaired
vetorans, gathered together on odd evenings, much {o
the amusement of the habitues of the adjoining billiard
room where the ‘hells of fellows’ made jokes at the
expense of the Gaels who passed through to the classes.
A little effort to develop within these walls wag unsuc-
cessful. It is interesting now to recall a ‘ recollection.’
At that time the Irish dances were absolutely unknown
in Cork, and an occasional dance (once a year) was
tolerated in the Y. M. S. under the disguise of a
Promenade Concert. The Programme was, of course,
made up of all foreign dances. The late Monsignor
Maguire put his foot down on these, Zl:ll(l he })eggcd of
the young men to revive the beautiful Ir}sh figure
dances, and promised them unlimited privileges if
they did so; but he begged in vain, for we are ‘tony
down South in some of the forty-six grades of Sociefy.
The Irish dances are still unknown in the C.Y.M.S.
What happened in Cork happened all over Ireland
when the LEADER came on the sceme. It seemed as if
up to then we had gone about with our eyes shut, and
our minds unused. You met a friend of a night, and
the first question was: ‘ Have you seen the new paper,
Tae Leaper? If you had not, he surely had the cur-
rent number in his pocket, and you got it. Wherever
half-a-dozen young fellows gathered cogether the con-
versation turned on the Leaper. Heads were put
together—the hearts were touched—the patriotism so
long dormant was awakened, and we all felt that the
chance had come for each one of us ‘to do’ something.
We must have a branch of the Gaelic League. We
must learn our language. We must have Irish manu-
factured goods.  And so the branches were started
here, there and everywhere. The writer was then an
‘exile’ in a hopelessly Anglicised town above Dublin.
There, nothing could thrive but bullocks, and billiard
rooms, and betting houses. Yet there were a few whom
the Leaper inspired, and the class was formed. There
was a sterling priest in the town whose presence at the
class would have great weight, and the writer en-
deavoured to secure him. So hopeless, however, was
the Sasapc that he laughed sarcastically at the pos-
sibility of any such class’ lasting longer than three
weelks, and he said good humouredly: ¢ What! a class to
teach Trish, you’ll have a hundred the first night,
cighty the next, thirty the next, and then S Oh
no,” he continued, ‘ we are great at a dash, but have no
staying powers.” Then to conciliate me he promised :
“If you have anyone in your class in four weeks, I’ll
go over.” THe had not reckoned on the power and in-
fluence of the Leaper; he did not anticipate that
memorable evening that in a few weeks he himself
would be consumed with the ‘spirit of the LEeapEwr,”
and he did not dream then of the two factories that
have since sprung up in his town, originated by his
onthusiasm, the outcome of the LEADER’S inspiration.
When we heard that ¢ Father —— had gone round
every shop in the town looking for Irish shaving soap
and couldn’t get it,” we knew he, too, was ¢ touched.” ’

reached us on Monday. The Report, as our readers
kuow, is issued by the Department. 'The Report, with
appendices, runs to 135 pages; the first Report dealing
with 1904 contained only 94 pages. We have had time
only to glance through part of it. These voports are
of solid value to the country, and are well worth to the
country whatever expense and trouble they may entail.
Apparently the trade of 1905 differed in no startling
way from that of 1904. Iowever, there is a great deal

The Wexford papers of last week-—at least those we
have seen—are very much taken up with Sir Thomas
Esmonde’s retreat to the Martynette party. The
novelty has worn off the matter now, and we only pro-
pose to make a few passing veferences to it. The pro-
nunciation of the motto, Sinn Fein, whick the Mar-
tynettes have attempted to grab as if it were an evicted
farm, stili offers dilficulties to the many unacquainted
with Irish phonetics. In the course of a report of a
meeting of the Gorey Board of Guardians, at which a
motion in support of the Irish Party was carried unani-
mously, we read at the end of one of the speeches:—
“In conclusion he would say, from ‘sin fin’ and all
other sins may the Lord deliver tkem.”

The Wexford ““ Free Press’’ states that Sir Thomas
continues to be a magistrate, and that he ‘‘took his
place amongst the other high and mighty administrators
of the laws of England at Gorey Petty Sessions last
week.”” What does Mr. Martyn’s ‘‘ National Council”’
intend to do? TIs one of their number to be permitted
to sit on the Gorey Petty Sessions bench?

Monday night last was the first night of the Oireach-
tas. It was, of course, Bank Holiday night, and the
streets of the city were crowded. There was, we under-
stand, a great gathering of people at the ‘‘Interna-
ticnal 7’ ExkLibition ; we don’t think we ever saw more
Tommy Atkins in O’Connell Street; the trams were
crowded, and several people in the streets, almost need-
less to say, were the worse of drink, but then a gref\b
many tourists on the bounce added to the throngs in

Dublin. The city was a hive of life—good, bqd, and
indifferent. The Round Room, Rotunda, at which the
proceedings of the opening night of the Oireachtas took
place, was not by any means packed, though tke atter'ld—
ance was good.  We could not help contrasting Irish
Ireland in the Rotunda and the sweltering mass of Irish
—Anglicised Irish—humanity outside.

The Gaclic League has only scratched its problems
yeb. That is a fact that we never allowed to escape
from our consciousness, but a great many people carried
off their heads by enthusiasm—as many people are prone
to be carried off—assumed that the battles were won
oven years ago. The miscalculation led them into tac-
tics that we fear did not strengthen the cause. The
Rctunda in Dublin on Monday night, as against all that
was outside the Rotunda, provided a contrast that may
steady emotional persons, and give them a truer per-
speclive of Irish Ireland in Ireland.

During the last seven years of work a great deal has
been done, and we believe the flowing tide, though the
cross currents and back wash are strong, is with the
Trish Treland movement. There are a great deal of dis-
tractions to contend against. The Martynette political
party has been a distraction to the Gaelic League. Time
was when the Gaelic League was the movement amongst
all Trish TIrelanders; now the little section of Mar-
tynettes, no doubt, regard the Gaelic League
sympathetically as a trump in their ‘“ policy ’’ of cards.
The Gaelic League officially cannot say this to the Mar-
tynettes, but we are quite free to do so, and so we say
it. The mixing up of the Martynettes with the Gaelic
League in the minds of many more or less uninformed
oulsiders tends to do no good to the Gaelic League
cither.  We regret that Monday night’s proceedings
were not marked witk more enthusiasm, and that the
Reund Reora was not packed, but we are not surprised.
T'he Gaelic League has to see to it that the Irish Ireland
movement does not go ahead almost altogether cutside
the League rather than develop within it.

offices on Monday, and the sight of a couple of mounted
policemen and a carriage equipment that included an
outrider, suggested the Lord Lieutenant, and so it

THE DEPARTMENT IN BEING.

VI.—THE MUNSTER INSTI:'L‘UTE,

HE Munster Institute is for girls what the Albert
Farm at Glasnevin is for young men.  The

drive up the long avenue, indeed, recalls the drive up
the avenue of the Glasnevin Farm. The original build-
ing, which stands in about 120 acres, is a fine s_ohd
structure, but large additions have been made since
the Department took over the place. Many of the
public remember something about these additions, for
there was, we believe, ‘a controversy and a row about
them. We understand that £10,000 were spent on the
additions to the buildings. :
The place was once called the Model Farm, then it
developed into the Munster Dairy School, and now it is
settled permanently into the Munster Institute. It is
delightfully situated about two miles from Cork City
overlooking the Lee. Somewhere about the year 1850

the Model Farm was started. In the then state of the
counfry and of the Land Laws, or want of Land Laws,
one can guess the sort of picture a model farm presented
to the mind’s eye of the country. One can imagine the

~ olass of people who went there for instruction.
didn’t make much history anyway.

It

390

. The Department took over the place about four years
ago, and new masters, new rules. There is an old
retainer there familiarly known as James. James was
born before there was such a hub-bub about microbes
and things as there is now. One of the girls at the
Institute recently remarked to James that the cow-house
was very clean. ‘‘Arrah, miss,”’ said James, as he
rested for a moment on his broom, ‘“ Arrah, miss, sure
there’s nothing but claneness undher the Department.’”

It was a boiling day when we arrived in company
with Mr. J. T. Carroll, the Sccrotary of the County
Cork Agricultural Committee. ~Mr. Carroll knows
his way about in hot weather when he is near a dairy—
so we were quickly cooled and refreshed by glasses of
delightful buttermilk. Miss  Sheedy, the Dairy
Instructress, was making a Cheddar cheecse when we
arrived, a dozen interested pupils were ranged round
looking at the cheese-making—perhaps giving an odd
squint at the visitors. Miss Sheedy had a hot iron in
her hand for testing for acidity or something of that
sort. Cheese-making is practically an unknown in-
dustry in Ireland, and why the case is so it would take
persons more expert than we are to explain. Probably
there are many reasons, and the chief reason may be
the lack of initiative in the country. If our grand-
fathers did not make cheese, why and the rest.
We gathered that a few minutes might spoil a cheese,
so that-it requires acute punctuality in the making of
it. Perhaps any product that might be spoiled by a
few hours, not to talk of a few minutes, is not fit for
our punctual people in their present stage of develop-
ment. If cheese-making did nothing but teach farmers
the value of minutes, it would be worth developing.

There are fifty girls living in the Munster Institute,
and- there are always about 150 to 200 waiting their
turn to get in; so the Munster Institute is, in one
respect, like the first performance at a popular theatre.
All the girls are taught to make butter in churns and
by the aid of small separators, as well as on the big
machinery scale. A wandering Gaelic bard might be
inspired to write a new version of Cailin ‘Oeap Crinci
na mbo6 did his travels take him round the grounds
of the Munster Institute, for each of the students has
to take her turn milking the cows.

The object of the Munster Institute is not, primarily,
the production of teachers, however it may be with
the individual pupils who enter it. But one of the
side lines of the Munster Institute is the training of
skilled teachers. During the four years that the Mun-
ster Institute has existed, less than forty teachers have
been turned out, or an average of about 10 teachers to
each year, and about 150 girls pass yearly through the
school. There are four terms in the year, and the
usual course for a girl is two terms. No girl but an
aspirant for teachership is kept for a third course;
but those who enter a third, or even a fourth, term are
not necessarily successful in going on, or heing kept on;
for the ranks of the aspirants for teaching are weeded
carefully, and only the best get through.

Each girl is taught cooking, dairying, ncedlework,
laundry- work, poultry keeping, and serving at table;
they have to feed the calves as well as milk the cows;
and we suppose they have to keep an eye on the pigs
also. At the Glasnevin Farm we saw a litter of ten-
days’-old pigs, but at the Munster Institute we saw
five little pigs only two-and-a-half days old; they had
the bodies of diminutive greyhounds, but their heads
were so abnormally swelled in comparison with their
bodies that they might have been very young Irish
Statesmen of the present generation. James was
spoon-feeding them with milk. James’ heart ovidently
went out to the helpless little pigs. ¢ You appear to
be very fond of them,” we remarked. “ And why

of what he is placed above,” added James, as he spilled

every day, and he.is an asset of the Munster Institute,
oven though the ‘claneness’ of the Department may

The poultry section at the Munster Institute, under
the direction of Miss Murphy, is on a big scale, much
bigger we should say than at Glasnevin. Perhaps
poultry rearing is more of a woman’s than a man’s
work ; anyway, we understand that the lordly male in
this island entertains a projudice against the industry.
There are all sorts of breeds of hens, all sorts of hen
houses, incubators, and in view of the fact that Treland
exported, in 1904, eggs to the value of £2,188,104, and
that the figure for 1905 shows a substantial increase on
that, the egg industry alone, not to talk of the chickens
—and the old hens for soup—is a big Irish industry
worthy of all the encouragement and help that instruc-
tion and science can give to it.

We found the cookery department in full swing under
the superintendence of Miss Laird, and all sorts of jams,
cakes, and things—enough, one would think, to provi-
sion a small town—were being made, or had been made.
But the pupils have fine appetites, God bless them, and
we don’t suppose it is necessary to look for an outside
market for the products of the cookery department. As
in Dunmanway, and Kildare St., Dublin, there is, in
addition to the well-appointed modern cookery equip-
ment, an open hearth for dealing with turf-fire cook-
ing.  Tke general well-being of the pupils is looked
after by the matron, Miss Smyth, whose father has been
superintendent during the past quarter of a century.

Needless to say, in an institution where the  clane-
ness ”’ in the cow-houses tickles the humour of JSames,
the interior of the building is as polished as a Convent
school. The cubicles in the dormitories are enclosed in
wood, so that they are practically separate rooms, and
the recreation hall serves also as a hall where reels
and jigs might be danced ; whatever be the dances, tkey
dance there anyway.

The fees for the Muuster Institute are very small—only
three guineas for tuition, hoard and lodging per session
of about eleven weeks’ duration—no wonder there is a
constant 150 or 200 waiting their turn for admittance.
It is a question of first come frst served for those who
wish to enter the Munster Institute. No class is ex-
cluded, and the daughters of city parents as well as
those of farmers are entitled to go througl: the courses.

DUBLIN CAMEQS.

III.—THE STATUES.

N the matter of sculpture, Dublin is certainly very
lucky. Not only has it a great number of statues,
but what it has are on the whole decidedly good. How
well off our capital is in this respect, only those who
have gone weariedly through the interminable streets of
London, looking at its statues, know. Dublin should
be proud of the fact, and might endeavour a little more
earnestly to keep the tradition alive.

Tt is the fashion of modern sight-seers who write a
book on Rome to despatch St. Peter’s in a few lines, for
St. Petor’s is big and common, takes the eye of the
mob, and is not to be fondled by dainty pens.

I must confess that to me the O’Connell monument is
the groatest thing in Dublin in the way of statuary.
It befits the man, his generation, his movement, and
the Ireland of new hopes that set it up. We could not
dream of such a memorial to-day, and the fact speaks,
more eloquently tlian all the statistics, of impoverished
blood.  The scheme.of the monument does not strike

one as interesting, but the simple massing of the
whole and the fineness:of the parts make it a worthy
thing, These four base figures are grand—should, in-
invaluable.

A town with no other statue than this should be rich—

The Goldsmith is very tender, very gracious. The
pose, in its imaginative rusticity, may be compared with
the courtierlike go of the Burke. And note the hoy’s
face on it. T must confess that I had been some time
in Dublin before T became accustomed to stop and gaze
ab it ; this statue requires noting, it grows on one. The
Burke monument seems somewhat commonplace.

One thing T may point out about all the Foley works
—the fine treatment of the draperies.  Foley could
take the heaviest robes in hands, and they came forth
from his imagination in bronze, well, not heavier than
the robes themselves.  Few sculptors could manage
heavy garments better. e treated them broadly, put
them into great planes, never forgot that bronze was his
material, yet always kept them light and pendulous.
The Lecky statue in Trinity College, or the Ardilaun on

+ Stephen’s Green, may be compared with any of

his works in this regard; and anyone who
can mark the difference will have got someway forwards
towards judging sculpture.

The general fault with the Farrell statues is that
they stand like people in photographs; they are too
obviously on their most heroic behaviour. Still they
are good sculpture. I liked the curious blending of
firmness with gentleness in the O’Brien, both in pose
and features; it is a good clean piece of workmanship.
The Gray statue is good also, albeit there seems to be
2 touch of Britishism about it.

Of the more modern statues, the Plunkett in Kildare
Street impresses one. A thoughtful, intelligent pose,
simple treatment, restraint in every part make it memor-
able. About it I must remark that it is very difficult
1o see; it faces the north-west; the light is generally
behind it ; one only sees a silhouette. Someone should
turn the statue south-west. We have only one statue
in Cork—the Mathew, and no one has ever seen it
properly since it was set up; and for a similar reason—
it faces north. How can people he so careless ?

The Mathew statue in O’Connell Street stands badly
in with the glorious company. One wonders whether if
the right hand were pulled down the left would not
tise up—the thing is so sticky. The Mooro statue is
irresistibly comic. It is comic in every part—hair,
head, face, hands, baggage—and the way the legs are
glued to the ground! A palpable joke! TLet us hope
the poor shade is too happy elsewhore to revisit tho
scenes of his youth; the soul was a sensitive soul.

HISTORY OF IRELANL.*

IN this, the sccond of three volumes of Irish His-
tory, the period dealt with extends from 1547 to
1782. The second volume is by far the most important
of the three because it deals with a time which exhibits
to us the earlier growth and final development of what

we may regard as the backbone of Irish History.
Some historians have, in their historical works, set
themselves the task, not so much to write history as to
distort it. They could not rid themselves of their pre-
dilections, and thus the influence of their prejudices is
easily discernible as a dominating characteristic of their

work.  Not to such a school does Father D’Alton
belong. In his preface he tells us that he has ‘‘no
thesis to prove, no party to defend or attack.”” He

has, he says, ‘“sought for the truth and told it, re-
gardless of what parties or persons might suffer.”’
These statements contain abundant evidence to indicate
the general nature of his work. Having separated him-
self from every obligation to maintain or destroy any
particular argument, our author is free to stand serenely
outside the sphere of strife, and preserve from begin-
ning to end an attitude of rigid impartiality. It was
Father D’Alton’s object to be impartial ; let it be said
at once that in his endeavour to attain this end he has
been admirably successful. Indeed it is the perfection
of his success in this respect that reminds me of the
one point concerning which I am inclined to find fault.
My cause for complaint is found in the fact that our
author has been over-scrupulous in his anxiety to hold
himself neutral, and thus he has become too exclusively
a narrator of past events. It is good that in History
the ““ even-keel *’ principle be followed now and then,
but even when such a policy is formally pursued it
should not, I think, be invested with that inflexibility
which excludes all little diversions by the way. Truth
is, of course, the first essential, but the most careful

-exposition of the truth should not be held to be incom-

patible with an expression of the historian’s own
opinions and comments. ~On most of the questions with
which he deals we are interested to learn his views;
but we are particularly desirous of discovering his atti-
tude towards those more important subjects to which
the clamour of controversy has given an especial
prominence. ~ When, therefore, we find a historical
work in which the events of larger significance are set
forth barren of the author’s personal opinions, is it
not natural that we have to confess to a little dis- °
appointment? 1In this case Father D’Alton’s reticence
is all the more regrettable as his abundance of know-
ledge would enable him to offer criticism of no incon-
siderable value; and, further, in this volume there is
ample evidence to show that Father D’Alton is emi-
aently qualified to pronounce an opinion in a moderate,
calm, and unemotional way that should at once place

hxm above the suspicion of partiality. Apart altogether
" from the general aspect of this question, it is only &

too obvious fact that we in Ireland cannot afford to
‘have a writer of Irish History, whose sympathies are

" Irish, submit himself to the process of ruthlessly sup-

‘pressing his opinions or even his feelings. What M.
' Dubois calls “ philosophic detachment’’ may be an
excellent spirit in which to write History ; but, in Irish
affairs at any rate, ““ philosophic detachment” is like
toleration—it ‘becomes an expensive luxury when the
other side doesn’t join in. When estimable gontlemen
like James Anthony Froude display an untiring dili-
gence in throwing bricks at your head you cannot be
considered very foolish if, at least, you attempt to show
some reason why such an unchristian practice should be
desisted from. If you preserve an angelic patience all
the time and allow your desire for impartiality to in-
duce you into an .undisturbed gentleness, spectators will
presently come to believe that the bricks are quite
fitting missiles and are directed at a quite fitting target.
With all respect then to Father D’Alton’s desire to
appear absolutely unbiassed, I submit that one’s repu-
tation for immaculate honesty as an Irish historian may
be attained at too great a sacrifice. There are attacks
to be repelled; there are misrepresentations to be cor-
rected ; there are ingenious but false arguments to be
combated ; is it wise, then, that we retire into silence
and leave the field in possession of our opponents?
Assuredly not. The unsupported assertion of what we
conceive to be the truth is excellent work if we can
persuade everyone to accept our word. The human
race, however, has not developed the habit of indis-
criminate credulousness, and hence it is desirable that
in disputed matters, at least, the statement of our con-
ception of the truth be strengthened by some demon-
stration of reasoning or by the production of whatever
evidence we rely upon for the establishment of our
belief. It is in Father D’Alton’s preference for
another course that I find my little grievance. I am
sorry that he elected, so to speak, to eliminate himself.
It would have been very agreeable if now and then he
had issued from the shades of neutrality and used his
oen more as a combatant than as an annalist. Ad-
mittedly he might thereby have run the risk of failing
in his desire to appear perfectly impartial. But it
would have been a risk with little danger because, as

+already suggested, Father D’Alton possesses those quali-
“ ties that would well enable him to break a lance with

an opponent, while still maintaining his character for
unimpeachable justness.

Apart from this criticism, it can well be said that
Father D’Alton’s volume is a most excellent work.
The author has displayed an admirable discrimination
in the process of selection and rejection.  Unfor-
tunately, some Irish Historians have frequently en-
tombed the main facts of a particular incident in a
mass of insignificant detail. This fault cannot be urged
against Father D’Alton. He invariably seizes the out-
standing features of his subject and presents them to

“us in a manner free from all obscurity. When, more-

over, our author becomes more prolix than usual, he
shows that he can be exhaustive without being dull.
It is a pleasure, too, to sée in this volume a complete
absence of that extravagant rhetoric which disfigures so
many works of Irish History. Father D’Alton’s style
is easy and restrained. He says what he has to say
without sentiment or passion; and his object being
neither to destroy the arguments of one party nor to
maintain the thesis of another, he finds no necessity for
the employment of those verbal devices which may
sometimes be clever but are always unsatisfactory.

In telling the truth and nothing but the truth,
Father D’Alton has done us a great service. We are
‘inclined to regard our struggle with England as being

. an uninterruptedly ‘glorious’ affair. It has not,

FERMOY.

ERMOY is, to all appearance, a thriving town -
situate in the valley of the renowned Blackwater,
and St. Colman’s College has made it famous in recent
years.
])age, to-day it provides a site for St. Colman’s College,
and also for a Loretto Convent, a Presentation Convent,
and a Christian Brothers’ School ; amongst the etceteras
ave a prison, a workhouse, a Tommy Atkins’ barracks,
and a Masonic Lodge. g

St. Colman’s College is a solid-looking building of
hewn sandstone with cut limestone facings—no importa-
tions about these stones. The College can hold about
150 students, and it is at once so national in spirit and
so successful that it is a pity it cannot hold double the
number.

Rev. Dr. Barrett, the distinguished President of St.
Colman’s, kindly came with us from Clonakilty to
Dunmanway a few days previously, so that it was only
polite on our part to visit this restless lion in his tem-
porarily lonely den at Fermoy on our way back to
Dublin from Cork. IHe looked none the worse for his
sprint at Dunmanway, and we harboured no resent-
ments, though he had outdistanced us. We roamed
the corridors and the class halls in his company, we
dined with him at the far corner of the deserted refec-
tory, and felt somehow as if we were diminutive crea-
tures at the wrong end of a telescope. There was a
third person present at dinner, Pdopaig Mac Suibne,
of Fermoy, a well-known figure in the Gaelic League
world. He complained of the heat, yet talked freely
and unofficially about men and things, including the
Gaelic League and Gaelic Leaguers. Later on we had
an interesting drive with him and Mr. Quinlan, during
which the economic incidence of the military occupation
in Fermoy was discussed by these two prominent
Fermoy residents. But we will come to that drive
later on.

Naturally enough St. Colman’s College had the air
of some banquet hall deserted on the day of our visit.
The young butler, who had the laboratory completely
at his service for the development of the results of his
photographic genius, was the only human being we
encountered through all the windings and halls of this
famous seat of learning. Six past bishops of the dio-
cese look down at you from the walls of the fine recrea-
tion hall—in the middle of the floor of which is a
centre piece bearing the legend Dilip 0o V14 agup
o’'Giunn. A portable stage quickly turns this fine
recreation hall into a theatre. The science laboratory is
a tip-top affair. When we were there there was no
sound to be heard but the drip-drip of water on some
of the photographic work of the young butler! The
laboratory cost over £1,000, the greater part of which,
in accordance with the general scheme at the time, was
contributed by the Department, and by the County
Cork Technical Committee. Back of the College there
is a great field in which are three hurling grounds. The
ball-alleys are in the front grounds facing the College,

- where the Fermoy Feis is annually held; and the

gymnasium is indoors in a hall to itself—the hall in

* which the Industrial Exhibition in connection with the
Feis was housed. St. Colman’s is a diocesan College,
and is not conducted by a religious order, but by
priests of the mission, and the teaching staff consists of
four_ priests and eight lay professors, excluding the
music teacher. The records of St. Colman’s College,
as we have had occasion several times to point out to
our readers, place it in the first rank of boys’ Second-
ary Educational Institutions in Ireland, and it is a
College where the Irish language is generally taught
and where the Trish spirit is fostered. St. Colman’s has
shown that a College cau be vigorously Irish in spirit
and challenge comparison with every competitor.

We had a look through Loretto Convent, Fermoy,
also. We understand that we had occasion at one time
to say something for its own good about one of the
Convent entertainments, but that is now a matter of
ancient history. The tendencies of all Convent schools
have, we should say, changed for the better during the
last six or seven years. At the Fermoy Feis, held in
the grounds of St. Colman’s College, several specimens
ol dress-making, fancy work and confectionery
from the students at Loretto Convent were
exhibited in the Industrial section. The Convent
schools at one time had to compete amongst themselves
for excellence in the production of accomplished
ladies ”’ ; now a saner and healthier tendency has set in
and the Convent schools find that they have to compete
in the development of useful Irish girls rather than
monstrous ‘‘ornaments’’ with “accents.’” Irish is taught
in the Loretto Convent and in the Presentation Convent.
The latter convent conducts the girls’ National school,
and the school is capable of providing room for about
500 girls.

There is, so far as we know, no industry in the town.
It is merely, apart from its educational institutions and
its Tommy Atkins’ barracks, a market town. We
were startled, however, to learn that some miles out-
side there is a woollen mill. Mr. Quinlan, the ‘“ Dark
Brother’” who runs it, is in an extensive way of business
in Fermoy. Although he is a ‘ Dark Brother,” he
is, locally, a go-ahead young man; and in appearance
and manner he is anything but “ Dark,” and he very
kindly drove us to his cave where he makes tweeds and
blankets. The mill is worked by water, and employs
about twenty hands. As the workers leave off early
on Saturday, the place was closed down when we ar-
rived, accompanied by the ‘‘ Dark Brother’’ himself
and Paopats Mac Suibne, but Mr. Quinlan had the
machinery set going for the benefit of the visitors. A
cyclist touring the roads would be agreeably surprised
to find this mill, employing about twenty hands, buz-
zing away in this unexpected rural corner of South-
East Cork.

‘Cné Coillead. Cpaobarge,” I have felt myself af

and spelling.” Reassured by which notice, the critics
who found fault with ¢ Sean Cainc,” for that it
over-emphasised the peculiarities of Waterford Irish,
will, I hope, extend to Dr. Sheehan's latest work an
unwatered welcome. The book deserves if.

Speaking gonerally, we can't get too much of the
peopla’s Irish into print. _If the language were out of
danger we could afford to pick and choose as between
what, of the spoken tongue, is worthy of being printed
and what is not. ' As things are, however, any Irish
the plain people spealk is, as a rule, better Irish than
anything the majority of our writers can compose,
and while this is the case, until, indeed, the exact
opposite is the case, we must print, and print, and
print what the plain people speak. Dr. Sheehan is
not befter known as a writer of, and authority on
Irish, than as a staunch advocate of this principle.
In the book under notice he continues his work, begun
last year on that principle. I only fear that nobody
except Waterford learners of Irish can fully appreciate
how helpful to the language movement that work has
already proved. We could do with a Dr. Sheehan
in every Irish-speaking district.

As I have said, the book consists of a series of
conversations, of descriptions of children’s games,
of folllore and of traditional prayers. For the learner
I should vote the conversations the best section of the
four. Most of us who have learned some Irish have
reason to remember with, what good results Father
O’Leary and “beipc fean” fixed on this plan of
conversations as a means of teaching their readers to
speak Irish. Inasmuch as Waterford Irish has its
own peculiarities, what Father O'Leary did for Cork,
and ‘“‘Deipe Feap,” for Kerry, is only now being done
for Waterford learners. And let not the learners of
hree or four years ago turn up their noses at the
simplicity of, and want of ambitious literary finishin,
hese conversations of Dr. Sheehan's. If they have
got beyond the infant's class, others have not.
Besides, as the book was made up with a special eye
to the requirements of pupils attending Coldiiproe na
Runne, it was a particularly happy thought to give
such pupils in these conversations an introdwction
hat will make them feel at once at home amongst the
people they have come to listen to and learn from.

As to the children’s games, there are some dozen of
them described in the book. I can't think of anything
hat non-native speakers of Irish are more in want of
than those games. A person would require a fair
knowledge of English to be able to understand the
Trish some of us speak as a rule. We can’t altogether
help that of course, but if we are going to transmit no
better Irish than that to our childven, theirs will be
a poor case. And the transmifting commences
immediately they are out of the cradle! The more,
therefore, of traditional child lore we can let them
have from the start the better. From the constant play-
ing of those games their very minds will get an Irish
twist that it will take a long time to untwist. Not to
speak of the idiom, vocabulary, ete. Nor of the fun! .
But there is no need to labour the matter. I venture
to think that, of the considerable number of young
couples who are now struggling to lay the foundation
of Irish households, if only for the sake of those chil-
dren’s games not one will be without a copy of “Cne
Coillead Cpsobaige.” - And, by the way, why should
not grown people, even though this is the twentieth
century, play these games too? Grown learners of

As might be expected, most of the folk-lore too is

Of the fraditional prayers, with the exception of
some half dozen, I don’t recollect having heard any
previously. I welcome the collection warmly.
Readers of the LieapER may perhaps recollect me from
time to time deploring that we should be dependent
on any foreigner for any of our prayers or prayer-

Seripture stories in Irish for our young folks. Doctor
Sheehan’s book doesn’t contain any such stories, but
many of the prayers are as suitable for children as
they are beautiful. Qur fathers were characteristi-
cally eloquent in prayer. Our children won’t be if it
is in English they will pray. Aye, or in translated
English for that matter.

I have quoted Doctor Sheehan’s note that he has
in this book—‘‘ returned to the literary standard of
grammar and spelling.” As regards the grammar,
the only editing of the people’s speech this necessi-
tated was (1) the printing of the proper cases of nouns
in the plural—the nominative being loosely used in
the Deipe for all cases plural—and (2) the omis-
sion of the aspiration of the “0” of “00’ before
verbs. The rule of the indivisible phrase, t.c., the
regarding of the constituent words of such phrases as
not to be declined—a rule apparently becoming more
and more general in Irish—has not been interfered
with. On the whole, the book shows that, leaving
out of account peculiarities of vlap, Waterford Irish
is just the same as any other Irish. I mention the
matter lest, as “Sean-Cainc na nOéipe’’ appealed
principally to Deisians and, after them, only to those
who wanted to get at the true inwardness of modern
Irish usage and idiom in their entirety, it might be
supposed that “Cn6 Coilteav Cpaobaige” had a
similarly limited interest for Irish readers. AsI say
such is not the case. North, east or west, as well as
south, the reader of Irish will enjoy the book; the
learner will find it in a hundred different ways use-
ful. . T need scarcely add that the serious student of
Irigh idiom wherever he may be will find it indispen-
sable. I trust it will sell accordingly.

OTHER EXHIBITS AT HERBERT PARK.

WO exhibits of an important Irish industry catch

I the eye on passing into the main entrance hall
of the BExhibition. They consist of two stained glass
windows produced respectively by Messrs. Clarke and
Son, Dublin, and Campbell, Belfast.  Further on the
Royal Irish Industries, who also exhibit in the home
industries section, Lave an attractive display of lace and
kindred products. This part of the buildings, however,
boing primarily a thoroughfare is not strong in the class
of oxhibits which invite much attention. The model of
the battle of Waterloo is, however, by the number of
puzzled people you see looking at it, one of the excep-
ticns.  Napoleon is there, and Wellington, and the
Tirench are just giving way before the conquering Eng-
lisk—i.e., Bnglish, Irish, Scotch, Welsh and Germans.
T don’t think I saw the figure of Blucller,_ however,
though rumour has it that he played a prominent part
in that affair. The Exhibition authorities are evidently
not so tender about the susceptibilities of other nations
as were those who sensationally suppressed the
“Mikado.”” And in these days of the ’entente tordiale’
it is perhaps fortunate that the model is a respectable
distance from the French pavilion. By the way, it
occurred to me that the Editor of the periodical show-
ing up the lawlessness of Nationalist Ireland often com-
mented on in the LeapEr lost a golden opportunity in
the Exhibition line. A model of the Ulster-Dutch vic-
tery at the Boyne would be just as convincing a re-
minder of tle century old fidelity of “‘loyal’’ Ulster
as ““ Waterloo ’’ is of the century old invincibility of
British arms, and then a few modern pictures of the
kind recently debated in the House of Commons would,
no doubt, add considerably to its effect.

There are many Ivish industries represented in the
Palace of Industries. The stands of Messrs. Hovenden
and Orr, Stephen’s Green, the well-known Irish manu-
facturers of hop bitters, non-intoxicating stout, etc.,
and other Irish firms, draw attention to the fact that
temperance beverages of the best quality are plentifully
menufactured in Ireland. TIn the interests of Treland
tLousands of ITrvishmen have from time to time faced
with a light heart the discomforts of a plank-bed, and
as many more, no doubt, would do it to-morrow if the
uccessity arose. The present generation, however, can
sleep soundly at night and rise refreshed in the morning
from an Irish-made mattress, with the pleasure of know-
ing that even in their sleep the industrial revival has
provided them with opportunities of nation-building.

It is now some five or six years since the Irish Curled
Hair Co. started the manufacture of curled hair, woven
wire mattresses, etc., in Great Strand St., Dublin, and
the progress they have made is alike creditable to their
business methods and the growing strength of the feel-
ing in favour of Irish manufacture. At their stand in
the Palace of Industries they exhibit various samples of
this industry. 3

Irish-manufactured candles are shown by Messrs. J.
G. Rathborne, and the famous Glanmire starch and
blue by the Silverspring Starch Co., Ltd., Glanmire.
Another well-known Southern industry in the manu-
facture of chemicals, colours, paints, printing inks, etc.,

is in evidence at the stand of Harrington Bros., of Cork.

Lven vhe precious motals can bo rendered more pre-
cious by the avtist’s hand.  This is the lesson to be
learnad from a survey of Messvs. Hopkins & Hopkins’
exhibit, a lesson made more ploasurable by knowing
that the most beautiful articles it includes spring from
Irish ideas and are the rosult of Ivish workmanship.
There are fortunately many mutely eloquent reminders
still extant of Ireland’s ancient artistic greatmess, and
amongst them s the famous Ardagh chalice now pre-
served in the Science and Art Museum, Dublin.  The
